Lynne Neagle AM afternoon Welcome Children Young People Education Committee received apologies absence from Hefin David Jack Sargeant Vikki Howells substituting Jack Sargeant Vikki welcome committee Item 2 eleventh evidence session Children ( Abolition of Defence Reasonable Punishment ) Wales ) Bill welcome Barry Hughes Chief Crown Prosecutor Wales Kwame Biney senior policy advisor CPS Iwan Jenkins head complex casework unit Crown Prosecution Service Cymru Wales attending looking forward views Bill questions Members Siân Gwenllian Barry Hughes Sian Gwenllian AM Thank law stands today
reasonable punishment protect children. Barry Hughes clear? law protect children? Sian Gwenllian AM : Yes. Barry Hughes range of offences by criminal law Offences Against the Person Act 1861 offences assault against variety people including Acts Children and Young Persons Act 1933 offences specific to children. general criminal legislation Offences Against the Person Act for adults children not subject to physical assault children section 58 of Children Act 2004 defence for person accused assaulting child to argue case acting impose reasonable punishment upon child
defence available for assault upon child contrast not available for offence involving adult could argued children have fewer rights than adults. Sian Gwenllian AM review undertaken by Government in 2007 stated retaining law in current form best thing to no evidence not working satisfactorily suggesting change since 2007. Barry Hughes law should reflect contemporary society evolutionary in nature. back 50 years types of behaviour socially acceptable many not now law changed to reflect don ' t need to go back long way to find for example
no offence as raping one married wife law changed to reflect not acceptable. from seen research 2007 review evidence opinions changing. research more current suggests young people find less acceptable to use reasonable punishment in chastising child than older generation. move forward people young now older adults of future. hope law change to reflect people behave within society. Sian Gwenllian AM : thank you heard evidence committee proposed law provide clearer legal framework take reasonable punishment defence make clearer for parents professionals. CPS have
view? Barry Hughes : make things clearer not sure make clearer if get into helpful look at examples stage prosecuting often case things t just black and white multiple shades of grey. Children Act clarity removed defence of reasonable chastisement for offences causing actual bodily harm grievous harm with intent incidents child cruelty under Children and Young Persons Act. matters clarified not entirely clear. series of Court of Appeal cases sought clarify law end of 1990s start 2000s European convention on human rights quickly
body of case law Children Act reflect direction case law. decision made early 2000s not to include common assault same category as actual bodily harm, grievous bodily harm. decision made back appropriate to do. wind forward 15 years seen research suggests present legislation section 58 applies to reasonable chastisement for common assault of children some interpreted smacking acceptable. t confusion. not as simple rarely case of being black and white degrees of grey. removing defence life clearer. Sian Gwenllian
AM : areas of grey refer to? few examples? Barry Hughes : example examples discussed child young child five - year - old child by side of road darts path moving traffic parent grabs hold child pulls back. technically taking hold of child is assault unwanted imposition of physical force. no - one say assault should come to criminal courts. utter nonsense. no hostility taking hold child to protect them. parent admonish child tells smacks child on legs. seen present law reasonable chastisement. depends upon circumstances happens frequently with child
size of child parent force of slap imposed. sits at one end of spectrum. move towards other end spectrum might repeated smacking shouting hitting child over head. spectrum of offending. one end spectrum move closer towards criminal. point crosses threshold from reported to police police take action move through tiers reach stage police think case should be prosecuted bring to Crown Prosecution Service for charging decision. decide appropriate means of dealing. absolute other end of spectrum decide s offence we think
serious should be charged brought before criminal court towards serious end of spectrum of behaviour described. illustrate one end relatively white other end black middle shades of grey? Sian Gwenllian AM : more prosecutions at softer end. removing this probably come on to that
